story:
Chamalis forced her face into a blank mask while the scent of scorched flesh crawled up her throat. The heat-whip—thin as a thread, merciless as fire—whispered and hissed, erasing skin with a touch. Time stretched until the head human snapped, “Back to duty.” The torturer coiled his instrument; a bony hand steered Chamalis down the servant hall. Her mother’s voice came low, tight with anger and fear. “Be careful. You almost lost control. If watching is too much, imagine the day you have to act.” Chamalis kept her eyes on the bare cement floor. She knew her mother meant to frighten her straight. Everyone said girls her age weren’t flogged. But everyone also knew too many girls left with Angheal males and never returned. A kitchen girl had tried to explain what happened after. Chamalis hadn’t understood the words, only the instinct that some fates were worse than death.

Her mother’s hand squeezed once and slipped away toward other duties. Chamalis went to the atrium to wait for the mistress to return. Hours of stillness stretched. Chamalis’s back ached; her feet grew numb. Then, just as the door slid into the wall, she stepped into the shadowed recess and fixed her gaze downward. The Angheal woman who entered was flawless: dark hair framing pale, perfect skin; pearlescent robes flowing to golden sandals; a sash of silken threads stitched with the household crest. She crossed the lobby without looking at the human statue by the door and disappeared behind an ornate panel.

Being unseen meant, for the moment, being safe. Both master and mistress were known for a bored cruelty. Their faces, still and beautiful, wore only serene contentment, as if even human suffering failed to stir contempt.

More waiting followed, quieter now, after the mistress had passed. Her mind slipped, unbidden, to the day officials had come to the single room where she and her mother lived. Her mother had gasped and folded at the knees. The man with the papers had droned, “Consider yourselves extremely lucky. Transport departs in three days from Car Park 435, Unit 76345. One bag each. Speak of this to no one.” His partner had grinned at Chamalis. “You’re lucky, kid. Most of your life in the Creator’s paradise instead of the rest of this hellhole, Earth.”

Tears now slid down her cheeks. She froze, hand halfway up, when she felt eyes on her. The master stood a dozen feet away, his gaze bright and tender, his voice like glass. “You will go to my bedchamber. I shall join you shortly.” He turned and strode off. Her legs moved without asking her. Terror didn’t even have room to form.

At the towering golden doors, a passing servant risked a hissed warning. “Do everything he asks. Don’t hesitate. You’ll avoid…correction.” The word hung like smoke.

Chamalis faced the doors and the truth so many had learned too late. The so-called paradise—city of the Creator, descended from the stars—was already full of the Creator’s first-made, the Angheals. They had welcomed humans only to serve as they served Him. Two billion souls on a ruined Earth dreamed of entering these radiant streets to escape hunger, filth, and violence. The lottery winners had merely earned a place in a smoother, colder hell.

She did not wipe her tears. No rescue waited in these halls, only polished cruelty and cosmic indifference. She touched the handle and, for the first time since arriving, prayed. “Please, let it end quickly. Don’t let it get worse.” Then she stepped inside to meet the prize she had been so lucky to win.
